section.title: Laws of Worship and Servants
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the Ten Commandments, God gives Israel case law, the Book of the Covenant begins here, showing how the ten words play out in the details of ordinary life: how to worship rightly, how to treat those in servitude, and how to handle violence, injury, and loss between neighbors.
section.thesis: The LORD instructs Israel to worship Him at a simple, unadorned altar rather than through carved images or human craftsmanship, then gives specific case law protecting the dignity of servants, the sanctity of life, and fair restitution for injury, embedding covenant ethics into the details of everyday disputes.
section.frameNote: Five movements: instructions for a humble altar of earth or unhewn stone; laws regulating Hebrew servitude and release; capital laws protecting life against violence; laws of personal injury and its remedies; and the laws concerning an ox that gores, closing the section.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “This is what you are to tell the Israelites: ‘You have seen for yourselves that I have spoken to you from heaven.
You are not to make any gods alongside Me; you are not to make for yourselves gods of silver or gold.

You are to make for Me an {{altar|altar}} of earth, and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep and goats and cattle. In every place where I cause My name to be remembered, I will come to you and bless you.
Now if you make an altar of stones for Me, you must not build it with stones shaped by tools; for if you use a chisel on it, you will defile it.
And you must not go up to My altar on steps, lest your nakedness be exposed on it.’
“These are the ordinances that you are to set before them:
If you buy a Hebrew {{servant|servant}}, he is to serve you for six years. But in the seventh year, he shall go free without paying anything.
If he arrived alone, he is to leave alone; if he arrived with a wife, she is to leave with him.
If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the woman and her children shall belong to her master, and only the man shall go free.
But if the servant declares, ‘I love my master and my wife and children; I do not want to go free,’
then his master is to bring him before the judges. And he shall take him to the door or doorpost and pierce his ear with an awl. Then he shall serve his master for life.
And if a man sells his daughter as a servant, she is not to go free as the menservants do.
If she is displeasing in the eyes of her master who had designated her for himself, he must allow her to be redeemed. He has no right to sell her to foreigners, since he has broken faith with her.
And if he chooses her for his son, he must deal with her as with a daughter.
If he takes another wife, he must not reduce the food, clothing, or marital rights of his first wife.
If, however, he does not provide her with these three things, she is free to go without monetary payment.
Whoever strikes and kills a man must surely be put to death.
If, however, he did not lie in wait, but God allowed it to happen, then I will appoint for you a place where he may flee.
But if a man schemes and acts willfully against his neighbor to kill him, you must take him away from My altar to be put to death.
Whoever strikes his father or mother must surely be put to death.
Whoever kidnaps another man must be put to death, whether he sells him or the man is found in his possession.
Anyone who curses his father or mother must surely be put to death.

units.label: Personal injury and its remedy
units.note: life for life, eye for eye

verses.text: If men are quarreling and one strikes the other with a stone or a fist, and he does not die but is confined to bed,

then the one who struck him shall go unpunished, as long as the other can get up and walk around outside with his staff. Nevertheless, he must compensate the man for his lost work and see that he is completely healed.
If a man strikes his manservant or maidservant with a rod, and the servant dies by his hand, he shall surely be punished.
However, if the servant gets up after a day or two, the owner shall not be punished, since the servant is his property.
If men who are fighting strike a pregnant woman and her child is born prematurely, but there is no further injury, he shall surely be fined as the woman's husband demands and as the court allows.
But if a serious injury results, then you must require a {{life|life for life}},
eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
burn for burn, wound for wound, and stripe for stripe.
If a man strikes and blinds the eye of his manservant or maidservant, he must let the servant go free as compensation for the eye.
And if he knocks out the tooth of his manservant or maidservant, he must let the servant go free as compensation for the tooth.
If an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox must surely be stoned, and its meat must not be eaten. But the owner of the ox shall not be held responsible.
But if the ox has a habit of goring, and its owner has been warned yet does not restrain it, and it kills a man or woman, then the ox must be stoned and its owner must also be put to death.
If payment is demanded of him instead, he may redeem his life by paying the full amount demanded of him.
If the ox gores a son or a daughter, it shall be done to him according to the same rule.
If the ox gores a manservant or maidservant, the owner must pay thirty shekels of silver to the master of that servant, and the ox must be stoned.
If a man opens or digs a pit and fails to cover it, and an ox or a donkey falls into it,
the owner of the pit shall make restitution; he must pay its owner, and the dead animal will be his.
If a man's ox injures his neighbor's ox and it dies, they must sell the live one and divide the proceeds; they also must divide the dead animal.
But if it was known that the ox had a habit of goring, yet its owner failed to restrain it, he shall pay full compensation, ox for ox, and the dead animal will be his.

altar.gloss: Altar, a place of sacrifice, from a root meaning to slaughter. God asks for one made of earth or unhewn stone, refusing the pride of human craftsmanship in the very place meant to atone for human pride and sin.

servant.gloss: A Hebrew servant or bondservant, typically someone working off debt for a fixed term rather than a chattel slave for life. The law caps the term at six years and requires release in the seventh, a limit rooted in Israel's own memory of unending Egyptian slavery.

life.gloss: Life for life, soul for soul. This formula, echoed as eye for eye and tooth for tooth, functioned in Israel's courts as a limit on punishment, proportional justice rather than an invitation to escalating private revenge.

basic.context: Right after the Ten Commandments, God gives Israel detailed case law for their courts to apply: how to worship at a simple altar, how servitude is to be limited and humane, and how judges are to handle violence, injury, and loss between neighbors.
basic.hermeneutics: These are specific civil and case laws given to ancient Israel as a nation under the old covenant, not a direct legal code for any modern nation or a New Testament ethic in itself. Their abiding value lies in the moral principles beneath them, the value of human life, the limits placed on punishment, the dignity even of servants, principles fulfilled and deepened in Christ and the new covenant.
basic.originalLanguages: God asks for a mizbeach, altar, of earth or unhewn stone, refusing ornate human craftsmanship at the very place of atonement. The eved ivri, Hebrew servant, must be released after six years, a term-limited status utterly unlike chattel slavery. And the formula nephesh tachat nephesh, life for life, sets a strict limit on punishment rather than licensing unlimited revenge.
basic.typology: The altar of unhewn stone, untouched by human tool, anticipates a salvation that comes entirely by grace, not human effort or achievement; and the limits placed on retribution here anticipate the fuller mercy Christ teaches and embodies, absorbing violence rather than repaying it in kind.
basic.christ: Jesus takes up the principle of life for life in Matthew 5:38-42 and pushes it further, not to cancel justice but to call His followers beyond strict retribution toward active mercy; and He Himself, the ultimate innocent sufferer, is struck without striking back, fulfilling the deeper righteousness this law was always pointing toward.
basic.god: God cares about the details of ordinary life, not only grand covenant themes; He legislates for servants' welfare, injury compensation, and even a farmer's ox, showing that His justice reaches into every corner of daily existence. His justice is also carefully proportionate, never permitting punishment to exceed the actual harm done.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 5:38-42 takes up eye for eye and calls disciples to a deeper righteousness beyond mere retribution. Deuteronomy 15:12-18 expands and softens these servant laws further. Leviticus 25:39-43 develops Israel's later, fuller vision of freedom from servitude in the Year of Jubilee.
basic.oneStory: God gives Israel real, practical law that limits violence, protects the vulnerable, and roots worship in humility rather than human craft. These case laws, belonging to Israel's old covenant life, point toward the fuller righteousness and mercy Christ both fulfills and calls His people to live out under the new covenant.

context.literary: This section, often called the Book of the Covenant, immediately follows the Ten Commandments and translates their broad moral principles into specific case law format, if this happens, then this is the remedy, a style common across the ancient Near East though distinctive in its theological grounding.
context.historical: These laws regulated actual disputes in ancient Israelite courts for centuries, addressing servitude, personal injury, and property loss in an agrarian society where oxen, pits, and field boundaries were common sources of conflict.
context.canonical: This is the first extended body of Israel's case law, later expanded and, in places, softened by Deuteronomy, and echoed by the prophets' concern for justice; the New Testament treats such law as fulfilled in Christ rather than binding in its civil form on the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To translate the Ten Commandments' broad principles into workable case law for Israel's courts, protecting life, limiting punishment to fit the crime, and building humane boundaries around the institution of servitude as Israel's ancient society actually practiced it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes specific civil and case law given to Israel under the old covenant, addressing servitude, injury, and property in the form ancient Near Eastern law typically took; it does not prescribe that modern societies adopt indentured servitude, capital punishment for cursing parents, or the lex talionis as direct legislation. Its enduring moral principles, protecting the vulnerable, valuing human life, limiting punishment to the harm done, are fulfilled and reissued in Christ and the ethics of the new covenant, not simply carried over as civil code.

debates.question: How should Christians read the servant laws in 21:2-11 in light of later abolition of slavery?

views.label: A regulated, humane institution distinct from later chattel slavery
views.case: Unlike the race-based, permanent chattel slavery of more recent history, this law describes fixed-term debt service with mandatory release, protections for wives and daughters, and consequences for abuse, functioning more like indentured servitude than slavery as later practiced.

views.label: A law given for a hardened context, later corrected by fuller revelation
views.case: Jesus explains that Moses permitted some things, like divorce, 'because of the hardness of your hearts,' Matthew 19:8, and many read the servant laws similarly, as a real but limited concession to an ancient economic reality, gradually superseded by the fuller ethic of human dignity taught by the prophets and completed in Christ, in whom there is neither slave nor free, Galatians 3:28.

debates.bearing: Both views agree this text does not endorse slavery as later practiced, and that Scripture's trajectory, fulfilled in Christ, moves toward greater human dignity and freedom, not less; the debate concerns how directly this text itself anticipates that trajectory versus needing later revelation to correct a temporary concession.

debates.question: Is the lex talionis, life for life, eye for eye, meant as literal physical punishment or as a legal principle of proportionality?

views.label: A literal penalty in some cases
views.case: Some instances, especially loss of life, verse 23, likely called for literal capital punishment, consistent with verse 12's death penalty for murder.

views.label: Primarily a principle limiting compensation
views.case: Many scholars note that other ancient Near Eastern law codes, and later Jewish legal tradition, generally applied this formula through monetary compensation rather than literal mutilation, using it chiefly to cap what a wronged party could demand, preventing escalating revenge.

debates.bearing: Either way, the formula's clear purpose is limiting punishment to fit the crime, not exceeding it, a principle of proportional justice that stands behind later legal traditions and that Jesus deliberately engages with in the Sermon on the Mount.

study.originalLanguages: The mizbeach, altar, God requests is deliberately humble, earth or unhewn stone, refusing the pride of human skill at the very place of atonement. The eved ivri, Hebrew servant, receives real legal protection and guaranteed release, a far cry from the permanent bondage Israel had just escaped in Egypt. And nephesh tachat nephesh, life for life, functions as a strict outer limit on retribution, not a license for excess.

typology.type: The altar of earth or unhewn stone, untouched by human tool
typology.fulfillment: A picture of salvation received by grace, not human achievement, fulfilled in the finished work of Christ, Ephesians 2:8-9.

typology.type: The servant who loves his master and chooses to stay forever, his ear pierced, 21:5-6
typology.fulfillment: A picture, drawn on by some interpreters, of willing, loving service that echoes the church's voluntary devotion to Christ, though the text's primary meaning remains its historical, legal function for Israel.

study.christ: Jesus directly engages this section's law of proportional justice in Matthew 5:38-42, not overturning its concern for justice but calling His disciples to a deeper righteousness that absorbs wrong rather than merely limiting retaliation for it. Christ Himself, struck and reviled, did not strike back, 1 Peter 2:23, embodying the mercy this law's limits were always meant to point toward.
study.god: God is a God of exacting, practical justice, legislating even for oxen and pits, ensuring no harm goes unaddressed and no punishment exceeds the crime. He is also a God who protects the vulnerable within the structures of an ancient economy, servant, wife, and stranger alike, showing His justice reaches into the most ordinary corners of daily life.

crossReferences.note: Jesus takes up the eye for eye principle and calls disciples toward a deeper righteousness of active mercy.
Later law expands and softens the servant release laws, requiring generous provision at release.
Christ, when reviled, did not revile in return, embodying the mercy this law's limits anticipated.
In Christ there is neither slave nor free, pointing toward the full dignity this ancient law only partially secured.

study.oneStory: God gives Israel practical law that limits violence, curbs excessive punishment, and protects the vulnerable even within an imperfect ancient economy. These old covenant case laws describe Israel's particular calling and are fulfilled in Christ, who both satisfies the law's demand for justice and calls His people toward the deeper mercy the law's limits were always gesturing toward.

quiz.q: What kind of altar did God ask Israel to build for sacrifice, according to verses 24 to 25?

options.text: An altar of earth, or of stones not shaped by any tool.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids using a chisel on the stones, keeping the altar humble and unadorned.

options.text: An elaborate altar carved with gold and silver.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids gods of silver or gold and calls for simple, unworked materials instead.

options.text: No altar was permitted at all.
options.note: An altar is explicitly commanded; its material and form are what is restricted.

options.text: An altar built only inside a temple building.
options.note: This law predates the temple; it envisions an altar in whatever place God causes His name to be remembered.

quiz.q: How long was a Hebrew servant to serve before being released, according to 21:2?

options.text: Six years, going free in the seventh without payment.
options.note: This fixed term and guaranteed release distinguishes it clearly from permanent chattel slavery.

options.text: For life, with no possibility of release.
options.note: The text specifically provides for release after six years, unless the servant freely chooses to stay.

options.text: Only one year.
options.note: The text specifies six years as the term before release.

options.text: No term is specified.
options.note: Verse 2 gives the specific term of six years.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of the formula 'life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth' in verses 23 to 25?

options.text: To set a proportional limit on punishment, matching the penalty to the actual harm done.
options.note: This formula functioned in ancient law to cap retribution, not to license unlimited revenge.

options.text: To encourage escalating private revenge between families.
options.note: The formula's function was the opposite, to limit and cap retribution, not fuel escalation.

options.text: To abolish all punishment for injury.
options.note: The formula establishes proportional punishment, not the absence of any penalty.

options.text: It applied only to property disputes, never to personal injury.
options.note: The formula is given specifically in the context of serious personal injury, following the case of a pregnant woman harmed in a fight.

quiz.q: What happened to an ox that killed someone, according to verse 28, when the owner had no prior warning of its danger?

options.text: The ox was stoned and its meat could not be eaten, but the owner was not held responsible.
options.note: This distinguishes an unforeseeable accident from negligence, addressed in the following verse.

options.text: The owner was immediately put to death regardless of prior warning.
options.note: Verse 29 specifies the owner is put to death only if the ox had a known habit of goring and was left unrestrained; verse 28 concerns an owner with no such warning.

options.text: Nothing happened to the ox or the owner.
options.note: The ox was still stoned even without owner negligence; only the owner's culpability differed.

options.text: The ox was sold to a neighboring family.
options.note: The text specifies the ox was stoned, not sold.

quiz.q: How does Jesus engage the 'eye for eye' principle from this passage in Matthew 5:38-42?

options.text: He calls His disciples toward a deeper righteousness of active mercy that goes beyond mere proportional retaliation.
options.note: Jesus does not abolish justice but calls for a fuller response of grace among His followers.

options.text: He rejects the principle as having ever had any value.
options.note: Jesus builds on the principle's concern for limiting harm while calling disciples further, not dismissing it as worthless.

options.text: He commands His disciples to demand even harsher penalties.
options.note: Jesus moves in the opposite direction, toward mercy and non-retaliation, not harsher punishment.

options.text: Matthew 5 makes no reference to this Exodus passage.
options.note: Matthew 5:38 directly quotes this formula, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, from this passage.

questions: God asked for an unadorned altar, refusing human craftsmanship in the place of atonement. Where might you be tempted to add your own polish or achievement to something only grace can accomplish?

The law placed real limits on how servants could be treated and required their eventual release. Where in your own relationships or work do you need to build in similar limits, rather than assuming unlimited claim on someone else's time or labor?
The formula 'eye for eye' was meant to limit punishment, not fuel endless revenge. Where are you currently tempted toward retaliation that exceeds the actual wrong done to you?

Jesus took this very principle and called His followers toward active mercy rather than mere fairness. What would it look like this week to respond to a wrong done to you with mercy that goes beyond what strict justice would require?

This law protected even servants and slaves from abuse and demanded real accountability from owners. Who in your own life or community has the least power, and how are you specifically using whatever influence you have to protect their dignity?

translationNotes.note: Pierce his ear with an awl marked a servant's voluntary, permanent commitment to his master out of love, a visible sign at the doorpost, the household's threshold, of a freely chosen bond rather than a hardship.

This passage is much debated regarding the unborn child's status; some read 'born prematurely' as a miscarriage with only a fine required, others as a premature but living birth, with the more severe 'life for life' clause applying if the mother herself is seriously harmed.

Thirty shekels of silver, the compensation for a gored servant, is the same figure later associated with the price paid to betray Jesus, Matthew 26:15, a striking and likely intentional echo in the Gospel account.
